title: About
text:
I believe that numbers tell stories — sometimes quietly, sometimes in ways that surprise us.
I’m fascinated by how data can reveal patterns that help us understand people, systems, and possibilities.
Most days, I’m working on problems that live at the edge of statistics, machine learning, and communication — figuring out how to make insights feel human again.

Now
I’m currently a PhD student in Statistics, exploring how modern methods in optimization and experimental design can make real-world decisions smarter and more efficient.
Along the way, I’m learning to translate complex statistical ideas into stories that inspire — whether that’s through writing, teaching, or building tools that help others see data differently.


Previously
Before diving deep into research, I spent time studying the foundations of data science, learning how numbers shape narratives, and building small projects that blend analytics with creativity.
That mix of art and analysis is something I still carry into everything I do.


Elsewhere
You can find me online here:

🧠 GitHub
💼 LinkedIn
✍️ WordPress
📧 Email

title: David K. Amakye
text:
Open Source in the Age of AI: More Important Than Ever Why openness matters even more now
Every few years, something comes along that makes the question of open vs. closed feel urgent in a new way. Right now, that thing is AI — and specifically, the large models that are reshaping how software gets written, how decisions get made, and how information gets filtered. The stakes of openness have never been higher. Here’s the tension: the most capable AI systems today are mostly closed. The weights aren’t public. The training data isn’t disclosed. The reasoning process is opaque even to the people running them. You interact with these systems through an API, and you take on faith that they’re doing what you think they’re doing. That’s a significant amount of trust to extend to any system, let alone one that’s increasingly embedded in consequential decisions. Open source AI doesn’t solve everything. Open weights don’t guarantee interpretability. Releasing a model doesn’t automatically tell you what it was trained on or what biases it inherited. Openness is necessary but not sufficient. But it’s still necessary. When a model’s weights are public, researchers can study it. When training code is available, problems can be reproduced and debugged. When the community can audit rather than just observe, accountability has somewhere to land. We’ve seen this play out already. Some of the most important findings about how large language models behave — how they fail, what they’re sensitive to, what they’re quietly optimising for — have come from researchers who could actually get inside the thing. That kind of scrutiny is only possible when something is open. There’s also the concentration question. Closed AI systems tend to centralise power. A handful of companies control the most capable models, which means they control a growing share of what’s technically possible. Open source is one of the few mechanisms we have to counteract that — to ensure that capability doesn’t become permanently gated behind a small number of commercial relationships. I’m not naive about this. Building frontier AI is genuinely expensive. There are real reasons organisations don’t open-source everything. But the direction matters, and the pressure matters. The open source community pushing for transparency, reproducibility, and accessible models is doing important work — not just for software idealists, but for everyone who’ll eventually be on the receiving end of these systems. We spent decades building an open web and then watched it slowly close. I’d rather not repeat that with AI. The time to care about openness is before it’s gone.

title: Curriculum Vitae
text:
Professional Summary
PhD student in Statistics with strong training in applied statistical modeling, machine learning, and experimental design, and hands-on experience translating data into decision-ready insights for business, research, and healthcare contexts. Research interests include optimization under censoring, reliability and survival analysis, and interpretable machine learning. Experienced with SAS, Python, R, SQL, and modern analytics and visualization tools.

title: Curriculum Vitae
text:
Education
University of Cincinnati, Cincinnati, OH
PhD in Statistics (Aug 2025 – Present)
Focus: Applied statistics and data science for industry and healthcare analytics; experimental design, optimization, and interpretable modeling under real-world constraints.
University of Texas at El Paso, El Paso, TX
MSc in Statistics & Data Science (Aug 2023 – May 2025)
Thesis track emphasizing statistical theory, machine learning, and applied data science; collaborative projects in predictive modeling and interactive analytics (R Shiny).
Kwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology (KNUST), Kumasi, Ghana
BSc in Statistics (Aug 2015 – Jun 2019)
Strong foundation in probability, mathematical statistics, linear algebra, and programming. Completed an A-graded applied statistics thesis developing a logistic regression model.

title: Curriculum Vitae
text:
Selected Projects
Optimal Experimental Plan for Multi-Level Stress Testing Under Progressively Hybrid Censoring (May 2025)
- Developed optimization-based experimental designs using Weibull lifetime models.
- Applied Variable Neighborhood Search (VNS) to improve parameter estimation under A- and D-optimality.
- Applications include reliability engineering, healthcare survival analysis, and industrial experimentation.
Prediction of Diabetes in Middle-Aged Adults: A Machine Learning Approach (Oct 2024)
- Evaluated machine learning models using symptomatic and demographic predictors.
- Identified interpretable and efficient models for clinical decision support.
Flood Mortality in Africa (2000–2023) — Arkansas Summer Research Institute
- Analyzed EM-DAT disaster data using Python and R to identify mortality patterns and risk factors.
- Applied statistical modeling and visualization to inform preparedness and mitigation strategies.
Dallas Airbnb Market Analysis
- Built multiple regression models in R to identify pricing drivers and predict rental prices.
- Translated statistical outputs into actionable market insights.
Weather & Climate Pattern Analysis (Michigan)
- Applied K-means clustering and time-series analysis to data from 58 weather stations.
- Generated insights supporting environmental planning and forecasting.
R Shiny: Regression Methods for Real Estate
- Developed an interactive analytics application to compare regression techniques and interpret results.
🔗 https://kingdavid.shinyapps.io/project_final/
